title: From SpaceX to OpenAI, Australians are piling into Wall St’s mega-deals
full_text:
Australians are scrambling for a slice of the technology and artificial intelligence-related deals flooding Wall Street, as investors lose patience with the lack of high-profile transactions on the local market and instead chase the flurry of mega-floats hitting the United States.
Elon Musk’s SpaceX has arguably captured the most interest, but Australian retail investors have had limited ways of investing directly in the company because the rocket and space data centre conglomerate is privately held.
SpaceX is expected to list in mid-June. Bethany Rae
SpaceX unveiled its hotly anticipated prospectus on Thursday and is expected to list with a valuation of $US1.5 trillion ($2.1 trillion) and raise $US80 billion, which would make it the biggest initial public offering in history.
Retail traders have piled into the only ASX-listed rocket exchange-traded fund – BetaShares’ Space Industry ETF, which has attracted $8.4 million of inflows in its first 13 days of trading.
While the fund does not yet hold SpaceX, the stock will be added two trading days after it floats because the ETF follows an index with a fast-tracking mechanism that allows freshly listed mega-cap companies to bypass standard waiting periods.
Rival ETF provider Global X is planning to launch its own space tech ETF in the coming weeks to capitalise on what it sees as a growing trend of Australian investors looking abroad to access lucrative AI and space companies that are unavailable on the local market.
“We’re starting to see Australian investors enter an exploration phase where they want to access themes that were once reserved for active managers such as AI, which makes sense given the Australian market has very limited exposure to frontier technology,” said Marc Jocum, senior investment strategist at Global X.
Only the country’s wealthiest investors have been able to gain direct exposure to SpaceX through family offices and private asset investment managers, who can participate in funding rounds or buy shares on the secondary markets.
BFA Global Investors, an alternative investment manager run by former Victor Smorgon Group executive Jonathan Belz and Gavin Ezekowitz, raised more than $US50 million from its wealthy clients this year to invest in SpaceX ahead of its merger with AI group xAI. The minimum buy-in was more than $US100,000.
BFA, which invests solely in US private assets, holds a $100 million stake in SpaceX and said it was seeing strong demand for its portfolio, which offers access to ChatGPT maker OpenAI and AI giant Anthropic.
“Australian investors and around the world are still incredibly underweight in these companies, so they’re allocating capital away from cash or other investments into these private companies,” said Ezekowitz, BFA’s chief investment officer.
“This is among the most exceptional periods of our lifetime in terms of how quickly these companies have grown, and investors are realigning themselves to a new reality.”
The euphoria surrounding the next wave of Wall Street giants hit fever pitch overnight when OpenAI announced that it was rushing towards a public listing and may file the necessary documents as early as this week.
Soon after, The Wall Street Journal reported that Anthropic was set to post its first operating profit. The company behind the Claude AI model is in talks to raise a new round of funding at a more than $US900 billion valuation, which would eclipse OpenAI’s private market value of roughly $US850 billion.
One of the few ways investors have been able to access the likes of SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic on the Australian sharemarket is through Pengana Capital’s ASX-listed Private Equity Trust, which offers a portfolio of global private equity investments.
The fund first invested in SpaceX in late-2020 at a valuation of $US50 billion, and the position has ballooned from 2 per cent of the portfolio to 14 per cent, based on its $US800 billion valuation. Pengana expects that figure to jump significantly at the next quarterly review of the fund’s holdings.
Private companies do not trade on an exchange, so their valuations are updated periodically around funding rounds, secondary transactions or other significant corporate events.
“We expect significant demand for SpaceX shares post-listing, particularly from passive funds once index inclusion is fast-tracked, as is widely expected,” said Pengana chief executive Russel Pillemer.
Pengana is also poised to launch a global private AI trust on the ASX that provides exposure to private companies across the global AI ecosystem.
Fund managers are finding other discreet ways to gain access to SpaceX, such as investing in companies that hold a stake in the space giant.
Jersey-based Contrarius Investment Management has built an 8.7 per cent position in Nasdaq-listed communications company EchoStar in its $3.2 billion Global Equity Fund, which is also registered in Australia.
EchoStar received a more than 2 per cent stake in SpaceX as part of a deal with the company in September. Shares in Echostar have doubled since then and are the second-largest position in the Contrarius fund, which is available to local retail investors.
